title: Jensen's Four AIs: How Cosmos 3 Closes the Physical AI Loop

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: You’ve never seen before what I’m going to show you. Most likely you have not even heard these words together. Omnimodal world model. What type of beast is that? Curious?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Welcome to Attention Span. My name is Ksenia and today we will talk about Cosmos-3, Nvidia’s newest model family for physical AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: When Nvidia first introduced Cosmos as a world foundation model platform, the idea was already larger than video generation. Cosmos was built around the needs of physical AI. It had video curation, tokenizers, pre-trained world foundation models, diffusion and autoregressive models, post-training tools, and guardrails.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The core idea was simple. If AI is going to operate in the physical world, it needs to learn how the world changes over time. I know I said it before, I know I keep repeating it, but it’s important because it’s so hard—

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: —for a world model to actually predict what happens next. They need to understand each and every little step of this process, and it’s a long process.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And you can totally see how important it is in self-driving cars. That’s as a human where you can see the importance of every little detail being worked out.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A lot of that Cosmos was able to do as a world foundation model platform.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But what if we move the idea further? What if we not stitch together, but actually unify all of this? World modeling, multimodal understanding, generation, action, and reasoning closer together.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: As one model family with shared architecture and different operating modes. This is the practical meaning of omnimodal here.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: What is the main architectural innovation here? Let’s see.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So how Cosmos-3 is built? Cosmos-3 uses mixture of transformers. What does it mean exactly? It has—

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: —two pathways, or as Nvidia calls them, two towers. The first is an autoregressive reasoner. It behaves like a vision-language model. It understands language, images, and video, and it can produce text.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The second is a diffusion-based generator. It generates images, video, audio, and actions.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And the reasoner asks: 'What is going on here?' And the generator asks: 'What should the world look like next or what action should be produced?'

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Physical AI needs both, and here we come to the natural question. Why are VLAs not enough? Video language models.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And a VLA is very useful, of course, it connects vision and language and action. The robot sees the scene, understands the instructions, and produces a movement. But this is still mostly the bridge from perception to action. The robot also needs to understand what may happen after the action.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Does the cup slide, tip or stay stable?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: If a car changes lane, how does the scene evolve?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: If a robot arm pulls a cloth, what happens to the object underneath it?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is the world modeling part. So maybe we can solve this all with just a world model?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The world model helps predict future states. It can simulate possible futures and expose whether a scene remains physically plausible.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But a world model alone does not automatically decide what action to take.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And it might not understand the instruction, the task and the agent's constraints well enough.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So maybe we need a world action model?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And yes, world action models are closer. They connect actions with future observations. They can ask, given this action, what will happen next? Or given this observed change, what action likely caused it? But many of these systems are still specialized by domain.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Embodied AI for terminal tasks, a model may work for driving, another for robot manipulation and another one for camera control. So the fragmentation remains.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And Cosmos-Free by NVIDIA attempts to reduce this fragmentation. It tries to make the physical loop complete.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: An agent sees the world, then it reasons about the situation, then it predicts possible futures and it connects those futures to actions.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then it acts and the world changes. So the model does not treat understanding the scene and generating the future as unrelated jobs. It lets them interact inside one framework. This is brilliant and very, very hard to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So now we should ask the obvious question: Does any AI model today combine all of this already? What's the novelty here?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The honest answer is: Not in one fully unified, mature, shipped system yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Until this week, basically. These capabilities existed in overlapping systems and still they were usually separated by architecture or product boundary or research goal.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And the complete unification is still the frontier.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: If we talk about a concrete models, we can remember DeepMind's Genie 3. It's very strong on interactive world simulation. It can generate dynamic worlds from text and let users navigate them in real time.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is a major step for world models, but Genie is mainly world model you can enter and interact with. It's not presented as a general multimodal reasoning system that autonomously decides and acts across robot embodiments.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Meta's V-JEPA 2 takes a different path. It predicts in representation space rather than generating every pixel.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is the latent prediction route. It asks whether AI can learn useful models of physical reality without spending all computation on visual details.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: PAN is an academic attempt to combine language-grounded latent dynamics with video diffusion. It explicitly connects latent space reasoning or imagination with generated visual world dynamics.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: VLA models such as P-0.5 and Groot-style robotics policies are very strong on perception, instruction following and action, but most VLA systems are not rich generative world simulators. They can choose actions, but they usually do not generate detailed future worlds as part of the same model interface.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So, if you noticed from what I described, there is also a deeper architectural fork here. One camp is latent prediction. This is the JEPA-style idea: the model should predict abstract representations of the world because not every pixel is useful for planning.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The other camp is generative rollout. This is the Cosmos, Genie and PAN-style direction. The model predicts by generating visible futures, often as video, which makes the rollout inspectable.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: This is actually one of the most interesting questions in physical AI: will general physical intelligence emerge through compact latent models that avoid pixel-level waste, or through generative simulators that let agents and humans inspect possible futures? Or through a layered stack that uses both or even something else? Cosmos 3 leans toward the generative rollout side, as you can imagine, but it also brings in reasoning and action. It's one more step to this layered physical AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And whatever the press material says, I would not frame Cosmos AI as another model drop. For me, it is part of a longer development. Cosmos started as a platform for world foundation models. Cosmos 3 brings more of the physical AI stack into one open framework. It is not finished, and that is the point. It is something to build with.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But to understand Cosmos 3, we need to talk about Jensen Huang's physical AI story, and the important part here is the structure of the argument. For the last few years, Jensen has been describing AI as a progression. First, perception AI: systems that understand images, words, and sounds. Then, generative AI: systems that create text, images, audio, and video. Then, agentic AI: systems that can reason, plan, use tools and act in software—a super active area right now, as you know. And then, physical AI: systems that understand the world well enough to act inside it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That last step changes the problem because AI leaves the screen, AI goes out there. And for Jensen, physical AI is not mainly about a robot walking on stage. The much deeper point is infrastructure.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Robots, cars, factories, warehouses, hospitals, construction sites.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Logistics systems, all of it need to be designed, trained, tested and improved before they act in the real world.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Saying robotics, saying physical AI we might think about one thing, we might think about humanoid robots, but it's so much bigger than that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And all of it means simulation, it means digital twins, it means synthetic data, world models, robot policies, edge computes, feedback loops.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And you see how I just name all of these things and if you read Nvidia announcements, it also feels like, you know, product catalog, Omniverse, Isaac, Cosmos, Groot, Jetson, AI factories, all of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But underneath this catalog, underneath all these names, there is much deeper thing. There is one consistent thesis: physical AI needs a full stack.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A model alone is insufficient. The robot needs a world to learn in.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A factory needs a digital twin before the real floor changes. A self-driving system needs rare scenarios before this scenarios kill someone.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The robot policy needs data and a lot of data has to be generated, simulated, curated or post-trained into being useful. Mouthful, but also this is why Cosmos keeps returning in Nvidia's physical AI story.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: In physical systems need to learn before they act, they need worlds to learn inside.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And those worlds need video, physics, time, action and, hear me saying that, reasoning. So that's what they decide to do in Cosmos 3.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: To be clear from the very beginning, Cosmos AI is not a finished robot brain. It's not a ready-to-market product that you can just put into a humanoid and expect it to work for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Cosmos 3 should be perceived as a builder infrastructure. It can become your perfect working horse, but you will need to work with it, you will need to post-train it, which you can do because it is an open model with code, checkpoints, synthetic data sets and benchmarks.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: You can inspect it, you can run it, you can post-train it and the best thing you can build on top of it. You can experiment. Of course open source does not make compute free.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: There is a bunch of ways to run this model family. You can use Google Colab, you can use Hugging Face infrastructure, you can use Nvidia BREV system or any other systems that can provide you with compute.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Or if you have your own GPU already, then you can run it there and basically it will be the cost of electricity.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But you can spend zero dollars on model access and start experimenting.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Now how to play with it? I will give you a short and unconventional way, because with all the AI tools anyone, literally anyone, can start playing with them.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Even without user experience interface.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So that's what I did. I have Codex that is connected to my GitHub.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then I prompted it. Okay, I need to set up new model Cosmos 3. Here's the guide.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Then I decided that I want to try Cosmos in NVIDIA infrastructure.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So I prompted, let's do that. And I gave the quick start on Brev.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But you can also use Hugging Face and Google Colab. It will be cheaper probably.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And you can just ask Codex or whatever system you use your GitHub to connect the models on Hugging Face and the system with GPUs.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But for me, NVIDIA and Codex combination proved to be the fastest and simplest.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I just literally talk to it and if I don't know something, I just ask.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: What just happened? How do I do that?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: If I want to do that, what should I do? And then it builds for me and explains.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And what was fine, Codex was actually tracking my spending on Brev, constantly reminding me to pay attention because on Brev, you pay per hour.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So then I created Hugging Face token because the model is on Hugging Face and you need to get it from there.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And then I did as I think of a brilliant thing, I asked Codex to create two separate playgrounds.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: One for Cosmos 3 Super, which is a bigger model,

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And another for Cosmos 3 Nano. I specified that I want to try both generation in all modalities and their reasoning part as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And it did that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: My partner, who has much more experience with coding, tried to do it all himself and trying to really dig into documents and see what's working, what's not, and why.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It took me much less time to work it that way.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Combining tools is the best way to do it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So when I play with Cosmos 3 and I tried video to video, I tried changing video, I tried creating video from text,

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I tried creating video from image and I tried reasoning explaining things to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So when I play with Cosmos 3, what I see is that it is uneven.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But for me it's not a failure, it's not a problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The most useful analogy here is that video with Will Smith eating spaghetti.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: No one believed that we can do significantly better than that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Remember those times? It's just like yesterday. Remember the thoughts, oh my god, realistic videos with correct movement? Forget about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And now, now we can barely see the difference if it's real Will Smith eating spaghetti or not.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So I think the same will be happening with omnimodal world models.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And maybe we actually should add the word action to the name, omnimodal world action models, or come up with some better name.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But in any case, I see an early open system entering the phase where builders can make it better.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: As of today, this is the least capable it will ever be.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: From here on, mark my words, the physical AI stack will only become richer, denser, more complete, and at some point, finally, working smoothly.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That's it for today. Thank you for listening. Tell me in the comments what you think will improve Cosmos three faster: Nvidia's own development, open-source post-training, better synthetic data, real robot data, or something else. What's your bet? See you next week.

highlights: Nvidia’s Cosmos-3 is an "omnimodal" world model family designed for physical AI, unifying world modeling, multimodal understanding, reasoning, and action within a single architecture.

The model utilizes a "mixture of transformers" approach featuring two pathways: an autoregressive reasoner that interprets the environment and a diffusion-based generator that predicts future states and produces actions.

While standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models bridge perception and movement, Cosmos-3 focuses on world modeling to help AI predict the physical consequences of its actions, such as whether an object will tip or slide.

Physical AI represents a shift from digital agents to real-world infrastructure, requiring a full stack of digital twins, synthetic data, and simulation to train systems for factories, warehouses, and self-driving cars.

Cosmos-3 is released as an open "builder infrastructure" rather than a finished product, providing the developer community with code, checkpoints, and synthetic datasets to experiment with and advance the physical AI stack.
summary:
Nvidia’s Cosmos-3 represents a significant evolution in physical AI, shifting from fragmented models toward a unified "omnimodal world model." Unlike previous Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that primarily bridge perception to movement, Cosmos-3 integrates reasoning, understanding, and generation into a single framework. Its "dual-tower" architecture consists of an autoregressive transformer for reasoning and a diffusion-based generator for creating images, video, and actions. This allows the model to not only decide on an action but also simulate the physical consequences of that action—such as predicting whether a cup will tip or slide—through what is known as "generative rollout."

The release is a core component of CEO Jensen Huang’s broader vision for physical AI, which extends beyond humanoid robotics into industrial infrastructure like factories, autonomous vehicles, and digital twins. Nvidia’s strategy emphasizes a "full stack" approach where AI must learn in simulated worlds before operating in reality. By providing an open-source platform including code, checkpoints, and synthetic datasets, Nvidia is positioning Cosmos-3 as a foundational infrastructure for builders rather than a finished product.

While the speaker notes that the model’s current performance is uneven, they liken this stage to the early, distorted days of AI video generation, suggesting rapid improvement is inevitable. The overarching theme is the transition of AI from software-based reasoning to embodied intelligence capable of navigating and predicting the physical world. Cosmos-3 serves as a bridge in this transition, offering a toolkit for developers to create more sophisticated, physically-aware agents through a combination of generative simulation and multimodal reasoning.
description:
NVIDIA Cosmos 3 is an open omnimodal world model for physical AI — combining reasoning, world simulation, and action in one architecture. Here's what that actually means, and how it compares to JEPA and VLA.

In this episode, we unpack what “omnimodal” actually means, why NVIDIA fuses world modeling, simulation, reasoning, and action into one Mixture-of-Transformers architecture, and how Cosmos 3 stacks up against Genie 3, V-JEPA 2, PAN, and VLA-style robotics models.
Plus the fun part: how I got Cosmos 3 running myself with Codex + NVIDIA Brev – no UI, almost no pain. You can too.

Attention Span is here to show you AI isn’t magic. Sometimes it’s confident autocomplete – and sometimes it’s an early, uneven, but very real attempt at building the world inside the model.

👉 Subscribe for more high-signal AI mechanics 
👉 More analysis: TuringPost.com 
👉 Interviews: @RealTuringPost

🔗 Links mentioned Beyond LLMs: 
- Nvidia Cosmos 3 on Hugging Face https://huggingface.co/nvidia-cosmos-ea
- Cosmos 3  tech blog https://nvda.ws/4wY4jzs
- Cosmos 3 announcement https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wSp6AiNIrsY
- Cosmos World Foundation Model Platform for Physical AI (paper) 
https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.03575
- What is Cosmos World Foundation Model Platform?
- JEPA and the Road to AGI https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z0fh0SY3VWc 
- DeepMind Genie 3 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDKhUknuQDg 
- π0.5 robotics policy https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Zn8yMaepzVk 
- Will Smith spaghetti, 2026 edition https://youtube.com/shorts/7zdVCQ52kMQ?si=qiPNTL9YzW_K9fhS

📖 Related reading on turingpost.com:

→ VLA Models Explained — Vision-Language-Action to VLA+: https://www.turingpost.com/p/vlaplus
→ What are World Models? https://www.turingpost.com/p/wwm
→ What is Cosmos World Foundation Model Platform? https://www.turingpost.com/p/cosmos

#nvidia  #cosmos3   #physicalai   #worldmodelsai  #omnimodal  #jepa #jepavscosmos #VLAmodels #AIrobotics2026 #airobotics  #NVIDIAworldmodel, #Cosmos3explained #turingpost
topics: Introduction to NVIDIA Cosmos 3 and the concept of omnimodal world models
The architecture of Cosmos 3: Autoregressive reasoning and diffusion-based generation
Physical AI and the importance of world simulation and foundation models
Comparative analysis of Cosmos 3 vs. JEPA, VLA, and other AI frameworks
Practical implementation of Cosmos 3 using Codex, NVIDIA Brev, and Hugging Face
